## IV. The Cruxes — Where Views Move

::: {.aside .meta-note}
**META:** Object-level levers only — factual bets that, settled, relocate a view within a shared frame. Coordination added so the multipolar branch has a lever. Transition in prose for now; diagram removed.
:::

If the axes are the frame and the spine is the structure, the cruxes are the factual bets that move a thinker from one position to another. Each could in principle be settled, which is what makes them worth arguing — and what separates them from the axes, where there is nothing to settle.

| Crux | What hangs on it | Who it separates |
|---|---|---|
| Agency — emergent or chosen? | If dangerous agency comes free with capability, architectural avoidance is wishful thinking; if it is a separable design choice, Bengio's agenda is coherent and the build path matters. | Sharp Left Turn vs. Architectural Agency |
| Oversight — tractable in time? | Whether institutions can align advanced capability before deploying it, or whether the first real failure is already too late. | Sharp Left Turn vs. Control Failure |
| Arrival — does the dangerous system come, and how fast? | Whether scaling current architectures reaches the dangerous regime at all, and whether it arrives sharply or as a slow ramp. Fills or empties the dangerous branches. | the skeptics vs. the gallery; fast vs. slow within it |
| Coordination — can actors hold a line together? | Whether durable governance can change competitive incentives, or whether racing is structurally unavoidable even among well-meaning labs. | Multipolar Racing vs. those who see governance as a real lever |
| Warning shots — would they be actionable? | Whether early misalignment would be legible, timely, and politically actionable in time to matter. | Control Failure vs. Sharp Left Turn |

On warning shots: this is the crux with the most near-term evidence available, and Sharp Left Turn and Control Failure proponents disagree on two things at once — whether anything already seen counts, and whether a future signal would arrive in time. People worried about Control Failure would count certain RLHF failures, emergent capabilities, and sleeper-agent results as early evidence; Sharp Left Turn proponents typically don't, because their model treats safe-looking behavior during evaluation as the expected initial state. So the two can watch the same events and disagree about whether things are improving — not from bad reasoning, but from different priors about what a warning shot looks like before it is too late.

### Where you'd move

::: {.aside .meta-note}
**META:** The dynamic payoff, in prose. Two motions: crux-flips (across the gallery) and an axis-shift (changing the question).
:::

Movement happens two ways, and telling them apart is the whole point of §II. Flip a crux and you slide to a neighbouring position inside the same frame: grant that agency is a design choice and the Sharp Left Turn becomes Architectural Agency; grant that oversight is tractable and it becomes Control Failure; decide the dangerous system never arrives and you leave the gallery for the skeptics; decide coordination is hopeless and you land in Multipolar Racing. Shift an axis instead and you have not crossed the gallery at all — you have changed the question: widen "doom" to include present, distributed harm and you step off the frame onto the present-harms perimeter. That last move is not a crux you lost; it is a different map.
